title: Writing to a file
instruction:
`Files.write()` is the simplest way to write a file:

```java
Path path = Path.of("output.txt");
Files.write(path, List.of("line 1", "line 2"));
```

`Files.writeString()` writes a single string. Both create the file if it doesn't exist, overwrite if it does.

Run this — then read the file with Files.readString.
successMessage: Files.write with StandardOpenOption.APPEND adds to existing content instead of overwriting. Files.lines returns a Stream<String> — .count() is a stream terminal operation.

title: Reading from a file
instruction:
Several ways to read:

- `Files.readAllLines(path)` — all lines as `List<String>` (small files)
- `Files.readString(path)` — entire file as one String (small files)
- `Files.lines(path)` — stream of lines (large files — lazy!)

For large files, always use `Files.lines()` — it doesn't load everything into memory.
successMessage: Files.lines returns a Stream — pipe it directly into stream operations. mapToInt + max + orElse finds the highest score without a single for-loop. Clean and memory-efficient.

title: BufferedReader for large files
successMessage: try-with-resources closes the BufferedReader automatically. This pattern handles files of any size — it reads one line at a time, never loading the whole file into memory.

title: Path operations
instruction:
`Path` and `Files` have methods for working with file system structure:

- `Files.exists(path)`
- `Files.isDirectory(path)`
- `Files.createDirectories(path)` — create nested dirs
- `Files.delete(path)` / `Files.deleteIfExists(path)`
- `Files.copy(source, target)` / `Files.move()`
- `path.getFileName()`, `path.getParent()`
successMessage: Path.resolve appends a filename to a directory path. getFileName/getParent navigate the path structure. Files.createDirectories creates all missing parent directories in one call.

title: CSV file processor
instruction:
Final challenge — no starter code.

Write a program that:
1. Creates a file `grades.csv` with 6+ student records: `name,subject,score`
2. Reads it back using `Files.lines()`
3. Calculates and prints:
   - Average score overall
   - Highest scorer's name and score
   - Count of students per subject
4. Writes a summary to `summary.txt`
hint: Use Files.write() to create, Files.lines() + stream ops to process. group by subject with Collectors.groupingBy.

codeChecks.message: Write the CSV file with Files.write().
Parse CSV by splitting on comma.

successMessage: A complete CSV file processor — write, read, parse, analyse, summarise. This is foundation-level data processing. Chapter 19 done!
